turns.content: I just implemented a simple counter using an atomic variable with compare-swap, but I'm wondering if there's a more robust way to handle high contention without relying on the CAS loop. My current code uses std::sync::Mutex and spins until the lock is released.

That Mutex approach will definitely deadlock or spin forever under heavy load because it requires another thread to acquire the lock before you can proceed. You are essentially relying on progress guarantees that don't exist when multiple threads try to increment simultaneously.

Right, so I tried moving to std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize but my counter still feels like it has a busy-wait problem if the CAS fails repeatedly. Is there a better primitive than just looping on compare-swap?

The issue with your current approach is that you are relying on progress guarantees from other threads to succeed, which is not possible in a wait-free context without complex algorithms. You should consider using the fetch-add operation provided by std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize instead of compare-swap.

Oh, I see! So fetch_add is better because it doesn't require checking if the value changed between read and write. But does that mean my counter is now truly wait-free compared to a lock-free implementation?

Exactly, fetch_add makes your counter wait-free by design since it never blocks or waits for other threads, unlike the lock-free CAS loop you were using before. You can safely call this in any thread without coordination.
That sounds much cleaner than my previous implementation with the Mutex and spin loops. Do I need to import anything special from std::sync or crates like tokio for this?
You only need std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize, no external crates are required since Rust's standard library already provides these primitives. Just ensure you use the correct type and trait methods.
Perfect! One last thing though, what happens if I try to implement a wait-free queue using just AtomicUsize? Can it really handle sequential access safely?

You can build a simple queue with multiple atoms like head and tail pointers using std::sync::atomic::AtomicPtr, but you must be careful because wait-free algorithms require specific memory ordering guarantees to prevent data races.
